Cup of Ambition · Kenya · Rwanda · Malawi · Brazil · Peru · Since 2023

What real change looks like
among smallholder farmers

Breakdown of patterns, themes, and shifts across Kenya, Rwanda, Malawi, Brazil, and Peru since 2023.

2,352
Total Farmers Trained
9

58%
Female Participants
Since 2023 · M&S Partnership

In 2023, Marks & Spencer launched a Cup of Ambition — a fund designed to transform the lives of smallholder farmers across their tea and coffee supply chains. In partnership with Emerging Leaders, the programme delivers Leadership for Life training: a three-module curriculum that reshapes how farmers lead themselves, manage their finances, and design new income projects. This is how impact looks like across countries.

Module 1
Lead Yourself
Mindset change & transferable life leadership
Module 2
Lead Your Finance
Savings, budgeting & spending discipline
Module 3
Lead Your Project
Design, plan & launch new income projects

Voices from the Field

How farmers across 5 countries transformed their lives through A Cup of Ambition programme.

🇰🇪
Kenya
KTDA · Momul & Nduti · Tea Farmers
Nduti · Feb 2025 Training
Beatrice
Tea farmer & juice entrepreneur · Nduti

The Emerging Leaders training inspired Beatrice to diversify her income, increasing daily earnings from KSh 800 (£6) to KSh 1,200 (£9). She wakes up earlier to tend her tea, cutting hired labour, and expanded her Back 2 Eden Healthy Juices business — infusing sugarcane juice with ginger and lime for health-conscious customers. She now leads a village savings group.

"I carried one of the flip charts from class and hung it on the wall in my shop. I use it to teach customers about cutting back on expenses and increasing income streams."
Momul · Feb 2025 Training
Naomi Martim
Tea farmer & young mother · Kericho

Naomi joined the training with just 3 chickens for home use. Inspired to start with what she had, she invested KES 2,500 in 30 chicks, sold her first batch at profit, and reinvested in two goats. By August 2025, she had 70 chickens, was saving KES 1,000 per week, and had become measurably less dependent on her husband.

"Inspired to start small with what she had — and never stop reinvesting."
🇷🇼
Rwanda
Assopthe / Sorwathe · Tea Farmers · August 2024
Assopthe · Rwanda · Follow-Up 2025
Murekatete Francoise
Tea farmer & mother of three · Assopthe

Before training, Francoise and her husband lost income through overlooked details — buying vegetables they could grow, paying for fertilizer while cows went unreared. After training, they grew their own vegetables (saving RWF 50,000/month), cut fertilizer costs by two-thirds, and rented out unused land. By September 2025, she had scaled to 400 chickens for egg and meat production.

"Her success has inspired other women in the cooperative to diversify beyond tea."
ASSOPTHE · RWANDA
Nirere Beatrice
Tea farmer & community mentor · Assopthe

Beatrice once believed her low tea yields were unchangeable. After training, she took a strategic loan to expand her farm from 525m² to 3 hectares, adopted professional farming practices, and dramatically increased production. Today she saves monthly, employs and mentors neighbours, and holds family strategy meetings every three months.

"She now views tea farming as the key to sustainable development — for herself and her community."
🇲🇼
Malawi
Sukambizi Cooperative · Tea Farmers · Dec 2023 & Jul 2024
MALAWI · POULTRY & VSLA
Caroline
Malawi · Poultry Entrepreneur & VSLA Trainer

Caroline used her COA loan to buy 14 chickens, rekindling her skills as a VSLA trainer. She now saves £4.72 per month — up from just £1.42 — and has catalysed 5 new VSLA groups, each with 25 women saving £2.36 per month.

"The training gave me confidence to lead others and grow my own business at the same time." — Caroline
MALAWI · AGRICULTURE
Amos
Malawi · Farmer & Land Investor

Amos invested £208 in 2 land plots and planted 200 banana trees (projected £3,500 in year 1, £10,400 in year 2) plus 380 blue gum trees estimated to yield ~£13,300 in 7 years — a long-term vision driven by the COA programme's business training.

"The training made me think bigger — not just about today's harvest, but about building wealth for my family's future." — Amos
🇵🇪
Peru
Frontera, Aprocoyce & Aprysa · Coffee Farmers · Feb 2023 & Nov 2024
Frontera Cooperative · Peru
Kely
Coffee farmer & beekeeper · Frontera Cooperative

Since the training, Kely has grown her beekeeping business from 10 to 20 hives, producing more honey and generating greater income. Her venture is fully self-sufficient — built with the right equipment and materials so she never needs to depend on others for extraction. Environmentally sustainable and entirely her own.

"A venture implemented to be sustainable over time — not dependent on anyone else."
Frontera Cooperative · Peru
Yaneli
Single mother of three · Coffee farmer & entrepreneur

Yaneli, a single mother of three, used her new financial skills to launch a gas cylinder distribution business with a £5,238 investment — achieving profit within three months. She also started beekeeping with her mother, sharing profits, and investing in reforestation. She now saves £6.40 weekly towards her eldest son's university education.

"Managing cash flow weekly — a skill from training — is how she funds her son's future."
🇧🇷
Brazil
Ascarive Cooperative · Coffee Farmers · November 2023
Ascarive · Brazil · Cheirinho da Roça
The Coffee Brand Couple
Coffee producers · Ascarive Cooperative

This couple had long dreamed of launching their own branded coffee product. The training gave them the courage to take the step and the framework to design the project. The result: Cheirinho da Roça — a new branded coffee label now on the market, built by farmers who finally believed they could own the story their beans tell.

"An idea they had long talked about — but the training gave them the courage to take the step."
Ascarive · Brazil · Inputs Cooperative
The Farmers' Cooperative
Group of coffee producers · Ascarive

Rising input costs had long burdened these farmers. For years they had discussed forming a cooperative to buy and sell inputs collectively — but never acted. The training was the catalyst to turn thoughts into plans, and plans into action. Their new warehouse is now operational, reducing costs for every member through collective purchasing power.

"The LFL training was the catalyst to turn thoughts into plans and now implementation." — Maria Paula Rocha, Ascarive Manager
In Their Own Words
What farmers say about the programme
"The impacts are immeasurable — beyond the projects, there is the change in mindset and life of each one present."
Maria Paula Rocha
Manager · Ascarive, Brazil
"I remember you telling us — we haven't brought money, but something of much greater value: knowledge. The programme has some special kind of magic."
Loison
Producer · Sukambizi, Malawi
"I'm particularly happy that our farmers are talking about mindset change. They are taking up leadership in their homes, families and finances."
Erastus
Factory Manager · Nduti, Kenya

🔍
Cross-Country Insights
What the data tells us when we look across Kenya, Rwanda and Malawi together. (Malawi figures are from its longitudinal cohort)
💰

Financial behaviour shifts faster than income and it sticks

Across every country and cohort, saving and budgeting behaviour shows the most dramatic and consistent change. Kenya moved from 8% saving always to 88% at endline. Rwanda from 41% to 95%. Malawi's longitudinal data shows it started at just 2% and reached 74% at follow-up, holding at 54% at endline. The longitudinal data confirms this is not a post-training spike. Nduti's save always rate was 91% at follow-up and remained at 89% at endline. Momul's budgeting reached 97.7% at follow-up and held at 96% at endline. Rwanda Phase 1 savings grew from £3 at baseline to £12.6 at endline. The programme is rewiring how people relate to money and the evidence holds across time.

📈

Income growth is real and post-peak dips are stabilisation, not failure

The longitudinal data tells an honest story. Rwanda Phase 1 income peaked at £65 at follow-up then settled at £60.5 at endline. Malawi's longitudinal data peaked at £84 then settled at £76. This is consistent with post-intervention stabilisation, where early behavioural intensity moderates into sustainable routines. The critical signal is that endline figures remain significantly above baseline in every single case across every country.

🌱

Tea dependency is falling everywhere

Nduti leads most aggressively, with tea as primary income dropping from 65% to 35%, meaning for most participants tea is no longer the main earner. Momul dropped from 81% to 53%. Rwanda from 99% to 74%. Malawi's longitudinal data spiked to 100% at follow-up before falling to 65% at endline, suggesting participants first deepened tea income before diversifying. The direction is consistent everywhere.

🌊

The ripple effect compounds over time

The ripple effect grows the longer a cohort has been trained. Kenya Phase 1 went ×3 at follow-up to ×5 at endline. Rwanda went ×5 to ×10. Malawi's longitudinal data shows ×13 at endline — the highest across all cohorts and countries. Community diffusion is not a one-time training event; it keeps spreading. The older the cohort, the wider the reach.

🎯

Attribution to EL training is near-universal across every context

Across all groups and countries, 90–100% of participants who made changes attributed those changes directly to Emerging Leaders training. Rwanda Phase 1 reached 100%. This consistency across different geographies, cooperative cultures and time points reflects the programme's perceived relevance and practical applicability in genuinely different farming contexts. It also provides strong evidence of additionality, changes that would not have happened without this programme.

🌍

The programme works differently in different contexts - a design strength

Gacharage is launching businesses, salons, shops, hotels. Nduti is commercialising and accessing new markets. Momul is shifting mindset from the inside out. Rwanda is expanding land. Malawi's longitudinal cohort has the fastest community diffusion of any cohort. Rather than producing a single uniform outcome, the programme unlocks whatever leverage point is most accessible in each local context, without changing the core curriculum.

📊

Cooperative Archetypes
Each cooperative shows a distinct change signature across Kenya and Rwanda
Nduti
The Commercialiser
Diversification gain+32%
Tea still primary35% ↓ lowest
Income multiplier×3
Income change£21.3 → £70
Loan payoff61%
StandoutMarket access
Moving beyond tea fastest — diversification is becoming the core, not a supplement.
Gacharage
The Entrepreneur
Diversification gain+37% ↑ highest
Tea still primary76.6%
Income multiplier×4
Income change£27 → £102
Loan payoff51%

Widest venture portfolio. Budget adoption at 99% — near-perfect discipline.
Momul
The Mindset Shifter
Diversification gain+31%
Tea still primary64%
Income multiplier×3
Income change£22.8 → £75
Loan payoff76% ↑ highest KE
Standout17% no-change
Deepest internal shift: from "stuck" to proactive. Foundation for further diversification visible.
Assopthe
The Land Expander 🇷🇼
Diversification gain+34%
Tea still primary60% ↓
Income multiplier×2.6
Income change£11.5 → £30.5
Loan payoff58%
StandoutLand expansion 36%
Land expansion leads over crop diversification. Family relationships uniquely cited as an outcome.
💡

What the cooperative comparison tells us

Each cooperative entered the programme with different starting conditions and has emerged with a distinct change signature, yet all four show significant movement across every key indicator. Gacharage's near-perfect budget adoption (99%) paired with the widest venture portfolio shows what financial discipline looks like when it fully takes root. Nduti's shift away from tea as primary income (down to 35%) is the most structurally significant. Momul's 17% no-change rate is the most honest data point from participants, and its high loan payoff rate (76%) suggests that those who did engage, went deep. Assopthe's standout land expansion outcome reflects a Rwanda-specific livelihood strategy that the programme unlocked without prescribing it. Taken together, these four archetypes make the case that one programme curriculum can produce contextually relevant, measurable change across very different farming communities.

🔍
Cohort 1 — Cross-Country Insights
What the data reveals when looking across Kenya, Rwanda, Malawi, Peru and Brazil together.
💰

Income growth is consistent — and the baseline context matters

Every country recorded real income growth, from Rwanda's 195% (£22→£65) to Brazil's 20% (£357→£429). The differences are not about programme effectiveness — they reflect where farmers started. Rwanda and Malawi had the most room to grow and grew fastest. Brazil's lower percentage reflects a higher starting point, not a weaker outcome. The consistent direction across five different economies is the signal that matters.

💳

Savings behaviour is where the programme punches hardest

Brazil's savings grew 690% (£10→£79). Malawi's grew 317% (£6→£25). Rwanda's grew 50% (£3→£4.50). Kenya's consistent savers jumped from 5% to 91% — a near-complete reversal of saving behaviour. For Brazil and Malawi, savings growth also outpaced income growth. For Rwanda, Kenya, and Peru, income grew faster — but the savings behaviour shift is equally significant: more farmers are building a financial buffer for the first time. This is financial management being rewired at the household level, and it will compound over time.

🌱

Diversification is near-universal across Cohort 1

84% of Kenya (Nduti) farmers, 92% of Rwanda, 82% of Malawi, 71% of Peru, and 66% of Brazil now earn from multiple income sources. In Kenya, this rose from 67% at baseline. In Malawi, 77% entered training as single-crop farmers. In Rwanda, 99% were solely tea-dependent at baseline. The shift is structural — it protects livelihoods against climate shocks, commodity price swings, and seasonal gaps. This is where long-term resilience is being built.

🌍

One programme, five contexts, one direction

COA Cohort 1 ran across three African tea countries and two Latin American coffee countries — different crops, languages, economic conditions, and cultural contexts. Yet every country saw income growth, savings improvement, and diversification. Kenya's climate resilience story, Rwanda's extraordinary knowledge diffusion (avg 14 skills shared), and Brazil's savings revolution all come from the same core curriculum applied with local intelligence. Cohort 1 proved the model is not just replicable — it is genuinely portable.

Longitudinal Follow-Up: Kenya · Rwanda · Malawi
Kenya groups were trained in 2024 and early 2025 · Rwanda group trained in 2024 · Malawi groups trained in 2023/2024 — all tracked across 3 time points: Baseline → Follow-Up → Endline. These are the groups with the longest tracking history, showing whether gains hold and compound over time.

📈

What the longitudinal data tells us

Across all four cohorts, the programme creates durable change that holds and compounds over time. In Kenya, Nduti income grew a further 79% between follow-up and endline, while Momul's ripple effect expanded from ×5 to ×6 — showing community diffusion still growing after training ended. In Rwanda, savings grew ×2.8 by endline and the ripple effect reached an extraordinary ×10. Malawi stands out with the highest ripple effect of all cohorts at ×13 at endline, despite income and savings dipping slightly from follow-up levels — a pattern consistent with post-peak stabilisation rather than regression. Across all countries, tea as a primary income source is declining as diversification takes hold, confirming that the programme is reshaping livelihoods, not just behaviours.
